coredata.dc:description: While cancer remains a significant health concern for humans, and is a serious and life-threatening disease, this research introduces an innovative drug delivery strategy employing platinum-doped carbon quantum dots (Pt@CQD) decorated with chalcogenide (O, S, Te) atoms for targeted delivery of the anti-inflammatory drug lapachone (LPC), which was analysed through density functional theory (DFT) at the ωB97XD/def2svp level of theory. Adsorption studies indicated exothermic behaviour, with negative energies for LPC_Pt@CQD, LPC_OdecPt@CQD, LPC_SdecPt@CQD, and LPC_TedecPt@CQD with corresponding values of −25.549 kcal/mol, −24.583 kcal/mol, −24.629 kcal/mol and − 24.419 kcal/mol respectively, indicating their chemisorption strengths. The quantum theory of atoms in molecules (QTAIM) and non-covalent interaction (NCI) analyses highlight weak and partial interactions among the complexes. Significantly, the engineered systems exhibit notable drug-release properties, especially LPC_OdecPt@CQD, where we observed a considerably slight physisorption and an adsorption energy of 10.277 kcal/mol and a protonation energy gap (ΔE) of 2.648 eV which balances effective adsorption and the ability to release the drug when needed. Furthermore, molecular dynamics and reactivity analysis revealed high stability and biological activity respectively among the designed systems, making them promising candidates for therapeutic delivery and holding potential for further in vivo and clinical investigations.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structural presentation of the lapachone (LPC) studied drug molecule.
Structural presentation of pristine, and chalcogen (O, S and Te) decorated platinum-doped carbon quantum dot (Pt@CQD) surfaces.
3-Dimensional maps of the HOMO/LUMO electron distribution of the complexes.
QTAIM graphical representation of the respective critical points of intermolecular interactions of the studied complexes.
Combine plots consisting of the molecular electrostatic potential map or distribution above and below consist of the non-covalent interaction isosurface plots of the various complexes.
Comparative analysis between the protonated and non-protonated complexes.
Tabular representation of the bond lengths (Å) before and after adsorption of the LPC drug molecule calculated at the DFT/ωB97XD/def2svp level of theory.

The energy of the HOMO (eV), LUMO (eV), HOMO-LUMO energy gap (ΔE), global Hardness (η, eV), global softness (σ, eV), electronegativity (χ, eV), electrophilicity index (ω, eV) and work function (WF, eV) were calculated for the ωB97XD/def2svp basis set.
Selected donor, acceptor, transitions, and highest E(2) kcal/mol energies before and after the interaction of the studied systems at the DFT/ωB97XD/def2SVP level of theory.

The tabulated calculated adsorption energies (eV, kcal/mol), basis set corrected adsorption energies, and the protonated adsorption energies, HOMO and LUMO energies as well as the protonated energy gaps of the complexes calculated at the DFT/ωB97XD/def2SVP level of theory.

The calculated topological parameters: Electron density ρ(r), Laplacian electron density ∇
2(r), Lagrangian kinetic energy G(r), Potential electron energy density V(r), Total electron energy density H(r), Eigen values (λ1, λ2, λ3), and Ellipticity (ɛ) of the studied complexes at the bond critical points (BCPs).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: While cancer remains a significant health concern for humans, and is a serious and life-threatening disease, this research introduces an innovative drug delivery strategy employing platinum-doped carbon quantum dots (Pt@CQD) decorated with chalcogenide (O, S, Te) atoms for targeted delivery of the anti-inflammatory drug lapachone (LPC), which was analysed through density functional theory (DFT) at the ωB97XD/def2svp level of theory. Adsorption studies indicated exothermic behaviour, with negative energies for LPC_Pt@CQD, LPC_OdecPt@CQD, LPC_SdecPt@CQD, and LPC_TedecPt@CQD with corresponding values of −25.549 kcal/mol, −24.583 kcal/mol, −24.629 kcal/mol and − 24.419 kcal/mol respectively, indicating their chemisorption strengths. The quantum theory of atoms in molecules (QTAIM) and non-covalent interaction (NCI) analyses highlight weak and partial interactions among the complexes. Significantly, the engineered systems exhibit notable drug-release properties, especially LPC_OdecPt@CQD, where we observed a considerably slight physisorption and an adsorption energy of 10.277 kcal/mol and a protonation energy gap (ΔE) of 2.648 eV which balances effective adsorption and the ability to release the drug when needed. Furthermore, molecular dynamics and reactivity analysis revealed high stability and biological activity respectively among the designed systems, making them promising candidates for therapeutic delivery and holding potential for further in vivo and clinical investigations.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: All data are contained within the manuscript and electronic supporting information (ESI).

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer is an intricate and pervasive category of disease characterized by the unchecked proliferation and dissemination of aberrant cells. Rooted in genetic mutations that disrupt the normal orchestration of cellular growth and division, cancer can affect virtually any anatomical region [1]. Its diverse manifestations present distinct symptoms, risk factors, and therapeutic modalities. The impact of cancer reverberates profoundly, affecting individuals, families, and communities on emotional, physical, and financial fronts. Cancer continues to pose a significant global health challenge, underscoring the ongoing need to explore innovative approaches for drug delivery. These endeavors aim to improve therapeutic effectiveness while mitigating potential side effects [2]. It also remains a formidable global health challenge, due to its intricate web of molecular complexities and effective treatment modalities. The quest for innovative and effective strategies to combat cancer has driven researchers to explore novel therapeutic approaches and drug delivery systems. Among the myriad compounds investigated for their potential anti-cancer properties, lapachone, a naturally occurring quinone derived from the lapacho tree, or pau d'arco, which is native to Central and South America has emerged as a compelling candidate [3]. With a rich history of traditional use and a foundation in medicinal plant lore, lapachone has garnered attention for its unique mechanisms of action against cancer cells. Lapachone has also drawn attention due to its ability to interact with cellular processes involved in cancer progression by interfering with the cell cycle and inducing apoptosis (programmed cell death) in cancer cells [4]. Its effectiveness against various types of cancers, including breast, lung, prostate, and pancreatic cancer, has been studied [5].

The pathophysiology of cancer is characterized by uncontrolled cellular proliferation, evasion of apoptosis, and the ability to invade surrounding tissues and metastasize to distant sites [6]. To address the multifaceted challenges posed by cancer, researchers have turned to interdisciplinary approaches, that integrate computational chemistry, nanotechnology, and drug delivery systems. In this context, the present study delves into the potential of chalcogenide-encapsulated Pt-doped carbon quantum dots (Pt@CQDs) as carriers for the controlled release of lapachone—an anti-cancer drug probing the nanoscale interactions that govern drug release kinetics. The advent of computational tools, particularly density functional theory (DFT) calculations, has revolutionized the field of drug discovery and delivery. Through in silico investigations, researchers can unravel the intricate molecular interactions between drug candidates and carriers, predict their efficacy and optimize their therapeutic potential [7]. Nanocarriers, particularly those based on carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and chalcogenides, have emerged as promising platforms for drug delivery due to their unique physicochemical properties and biocompatibility. Carbon quantum dots have gained significant attention in drug delivery applications owing to their tunable optical properties, excellent biocompatibility, and facile surface functionalization [8,9]. Additionally, chalcogenides, such as oxygen (o), sulfur (S), and tellurium (Te), provide intriguing opportunities to engineer the physicochemical characteristics of nanocarriers, thereby influencing drug encapsulation, release kinetics, and overall therapeutic outcomes [10].

Previously reported works have shown that the incorporation of platinum (Pt) into carbon quantum dots and other nanomaterials introduces catalytic and electronic properties that can further enhance drug delivery functionalities. Pt-doped CQDs have demonstrated exceptional potential in various biomedical applications including imaging, therapy, and sensing [11] which can be seen in the research work reported by Sha et al. on the controlled synthesis of platinum (Pt) nanoflowers supported on carbon quantum dots as a highly effective catalyst for methanol electro-oxidation [12]. The study demonstrates the potential of this catalyst in facilitating the electro-oxidation of methanol, highlighting its promising application in fuel cell technology. Pt-doped carbon nanotube was used as a carrier for the release of fluorouracil an anticarcinogenic drug by Khalili et al. Employing the B3LYP-D3 level, the study revealed that Fluorouracil established stable binding with Pt-CNT in aqueous environments, exhibiting adsorption energy of −1.12 eV indicative of a chemisorption process. Furthermore, charge analysis unveils a notable alteration in the overall charge of both the host and guest systems following the binding of Fluorouracil to the nanocarrier. Meanwhile, the interaction between Fluorouracil and pristine Carbon Nanotubes (CNT) was identified as a typical physisorption process, characterized by an adsorption energy of approximately −0.405 eV. The findings highlight the promising applicability of these nanostructured materials in drug delivery [13]. Bao et al. investigated the utilization of polyethylene glycol (PEG)-modified, platinum-doped carbon nanoparticles (PEG-PtCNPs). This study explores their effectiveness in impeding cancer cell migration, accomplishing laser-triggered nuclear delivery, and enhancing tumor ablation efficacy. The PEG-PtCNPs, characterized by a positively charged nature and a hydrodynamic diameter of approximately 19 nm, ingress cells through endocytosis. Upon exposure to laser irradiation, they induce cytoskeletal disruption, impede cancer cell migration, and facilitate nuclear delivery, all without relying on nuclear targeting ligands. This underscores their potential for targeted tumor ablation across multiple organelles, offering improved therapeutic efficacy with minimized side effects. [14].

Ogunwale et al. investigated sensors for detecting hydroxyurea and nitrosourea drugs, employing pristine C60 fullerenes as well as those decorated with Ir and Au. Employing Density Functional Theory (DFT), the findings indicate that Au- and Ir-decorated fullerenes exhibit more robust interactions with HU and NU drugs compared to pristine C60. Specifically, the interaction energies are −5.674, −12.767, −27.065, −31.423, and − 31.676 kcal/mol for HU/C60, NU/C60, NU/AuC59, NU/IrC59, and HU/IrC59, respectively. The addition of Ir-metal decoration enhances reactivity and electronic sensitivity. Topology analysis suggests stabilization through both covalent bonding and weak van der Waals forces. Consequently, the IrC59 fullerene emerges as a promising dual-type sensor for detecting HU and NU drugs [15]. In a recent study, Akshayan et al. conducted a first-principles investigation on the structural and bonding characteristics of Ni-group transition metal carbonyls containing terminal tin chalcogenides, denoted as [M(CO)3SnX] (where M represents Ni, Pd, Pt; and X represents O, S, Se, and Te) complexes [16]. Utilizing the density functional theory (DFT) approach (B3LYP/LANL2DZ/6-31G*), the researchers observed tetrahedral 18-electron configurations in the optimized structures. Bonding analysis highlighted polarized M-Sn bonds and distinctive sigma donor behaviour of SnX. The molecular orbital analysis identified trends in stability based on energy gaps, contributing valuable insights into the understanding of molecular structures, reactivity, and potential applications across diverse chemical domains. Further, Gholami et al. explored the potential application of pristine and metal-encapsulated B36N36 fullerenes for delivering β-lapachone anticancer drugs. Using dispersion-corrected density functional theory calculations, their findings demonstrate that B36N36 can host up to six drugs, exhibiting a binding energy per β-lapachone of −7.8 kcal/mol in the water phase. Furthermore, the encapsulation of alkali or alkaline earth metals (AAM) inside B36N36 enhances its adsorption performance for β-lapachone, with higher binding energies observed in both gas and water phases. The study also investigates desorption mechanisms, suggesting potential drug release in cancer cells upon protonation, particularly for B36N36 and K-encapsulated B36N36 carriers. [17].

Leveraging these attributes, our study investigated the intricate interplay between chalcogenide-encapsulated Pt-doped CQDs and lapachone through a comprehensive density functional theory (DFT) approach. In this article, we present a detailed examination of the electronic and structural characteristics, adsorption efficacy, protonation, QTAIM, NCI, bonding and charge transfer of pristine and chalcogenide (O, S, Te) encapsulated Pt-doped CQDs, providing a theoretical foundation for their application as effective carriers for lapachone. The controlled release mechanism is elucidated, offering valuable insights into the dynamics of drug delivery within a biological context. The anticipated outcomes from this DFT-guided exploration hold great promise for advancing the field of cancer therapeutics through the development of innovative nanocarrier systems tailored for controlled and targeted drug release.

In the present study, geometric optimizations were performed using Gaussian09W software [18] while employing density functional theory (DFT) with the ωB97XD functional and the def2-SVP basis set. The selection of this basis set was guided by its long-range polarization function and its ability to accommodate doped platinum metal and introduce chalcogen atoms on the nano-surface [19]. The choice of the ωB97XD/def2svp level of theory for DFT calculations was also motivated by its accuracy in describing dispersion interactions, efficiency in computational cost, and applicability to a wide range of molecular systems. Following the geometry optimization, we conducted a frequency calculation, which confirmed the stability of the optimized geometries as indicated by the absence of imaginary frequencies. The structural characteristics, such as bond labels and lengths, of interactions involving the drug lapachone (LPC) with both pristine surfaces and surfaces doped with chalcogens (O, S, and Te). This study systematically examined the electronic properties, via Frontier Molecular Orbital (FMO) analysis through Eq. 1. Global descriptors were computed based on Koopsmans' approximations, utilizing Eqs. (2)–(6). These computations were derived from optimized structures obtained using Gaussview 6.0.16 software [20].

In Eq. 7, Eads denotes the adsorption energy characterizing the interaction between the modeled surfaces and the lapachone drug molecule. This equation encompasses several contributing components: E(drug/surface) represents the overall energy of the system, E(surface) signifies the inherent energy of the adsorbent, and E(drug) reflects the intrinsic energy state of the adsorbate. Consequently, the adsorption energy (Eads) is elucidated by the interplay among these factors, where E(drug/surface) encapsulates the collective energy of the adsorbate-adsorbent interaction, E(surface) denotes the intrinsic energy of the adsorbent, and E(drug) delineates the inherent energy state of the adsorbate. The quantum theory of atoms in molecules (QTAIM) and non-covalent interaction (NCI) visual analyses were performed using the Multiwfn program package [23]. By utilizing diverse parameters to elucidate various complexes, the connection between electron energy density and Laplacian is interpreted employing the virial theorem, as demonstrated below [24,25].

Additionally, 2D NCI plots and the molecular electrostatic potential (MESP) maps were visualized using Visual Molecular Dynamics (VMD) software [26].

The structural elucidation of organic materials is crucial in density functional theory studies, as it provides the basis for determining the optimal structural conformation or configuration of molecules. In this study, we engineered a Pt-doped carbon quantum dot and surfaces decorated with chalcogens (O, S, and Te) that individually interacted with a lapachone (LPC) adsorbate as displayed in Figs. 1 and 2
, respectively. To provide a more accurate description of the system under study, especially when long-range interactions are important, as well as to precisely capture the interactions between atoms, we utilized the WB97XD (Winstein-Becke 97 with exchange and dispersion) functional method. This method is recognized for its effectiveness in describing weak intermolecular interactions, particularly dispersion forces [27,28]. These forces play a crucial role in systems such as van der Waals complexes, molecular aggregates, and noncovalently bonded systems. Additionally, we employed the def2SVP basis set, which was selected for its wide dispersion range and is particularly suitable for accommodating heavy metals due to its broad range. In all cases, all compounds were stabilized at the ground state by maintaining a singlet multiplicity (M = 2ST + 1 = 1) and a net charge of zero (Q = 0 lel) for interactions involving the pristine, O, S, and Te decorated surfaces [29]. The geometric positions of the atoms in the interacting carbon quantum dots were determined using structural parameters such as bond lengths, and bond labels. These parameters were considered both before and after the adsorption of the drug molecule, and the results are presented in Table 1
.

Herein, the bond lengths corresponding to Pt – C atoms on the pristine Pt@CQD surface before chalcogenide decoration and LPC adsorption were 1.930 Å and 1.940 Å respectively, and after LPC molecule adsorption, the bond lengths ranged from 1.951 Å to 1.974 Å. Furthermore, the bond characteristics within a compound can be used to estimate various properties such as reactivity, biological activity, polarity, phase of matter, and electron transfer strength [30]. A strong interaction and reactivity within a compound is often associated with short bond lengths. This suggests that in a compound or complex undergoing chemical interaction, the presence of short bonds after adsorption indicates high reactivity with an interacting species. However, it should be noted that bond length is not the sole determinant of reactivity in an interaction. The structural display of the interactions between the designed surface and the LPC drug is presented in Fig. S1 of the supporting information. Considering the effect of the chalcogenide decorated complexes on the adsorption of lapachone, we noticed that in OdecPt@CQD, the bond length between Pt – C atoms before LPC interaction was 1.961 Å while at the point of oxygen (O) coloration it was 1.480 Å. After Lapachone adsorption (LPC_OdecPt@CQD), bond lengths decreased to 1.956 Å, 1.957 Å, 1.930 Å, 1.475 Å, and 1.479 Å corresponding to the Pt35 – C6, Pt36 – C8, Pt36 – C28, O36 – C3 and O36 – C7 bond labels respectively. In LPC_SdecPt@CQD we observed PtC bonding lengths of 1.932 Å 1.970 Å, while CS bonding lengths of 1.773 Å and 1.808 Å were observed. After interaction, a decrease in the bond length which ranged from 1.931 to 1.967 Å and from 1.770 to 1.808 Å for the PtC and CS bonds, respectively. Additionally, before bond lengths of 1.952, 2.008, and 2.012 Å were recorded for PtC while lengths of 2.201 and 2.093 Å was recorded for CS bond labels in LPC_TedecPt@CQD. In essence, the observable increase and notable decrease in bond length seen herein after LPC adsorption depict changes in the structural activity of the modeled surface upon adsorption of the drug molecule hence projecting its enhanced biological activity and reactivity.

The HOMO-LUMO analysis is a pivotal aspect of molecular investigations. The highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO), which are collectively referred to as frontier molecular orbitals (FMOs), play a central role in understanding the stability and chemical reactivity of molecular entities [31–33]. The energy gap, representing the disparity between the HOMO and LUMO energies, holds significance for charge transfer and sensing tendencies within a molecular framework. A smaller energy gap (ΔE) is associated with highly reactive and less stable molecules, whereas larger gaps characterize compounds that are less reactive and more stable [34]. Herein, the energy gaps for the pristine and chalcogen (O, S and Te) decorated surfaces are 6.046, 4.949, 5.203 and 4.971 eV for Pt@CQD, OdecPt@CQD, OdecPt@CQD, and TedecPt@CQD respectively. The computation of the HOMO–LUMO energy gaps for the analysed surfaces and respective complexes (when lapachone is adsorbed) was conducted using the ωB97XD/def2SVP level of theory, as detailed in Table 2 . The distribution patterns of their frontier molecular orbitals are visually presented in Fig. 3
. After LPC adsorption on the surfaces, the ΔE reduced to 4.689, 4.754, 5.088, and 4.963 eV which corresponding to LPC_Pt@CQD, LPC_OdecPt@CQD, LPC_SdecPt@CQD, and LPC_TedecPt@CQD respectively. This result shows that the electronic properties and conductivities of the complexes change upon the interaction of the drug molecules with the engineered pristine and decorated surfaces. For instance, the ΔE, EHOMO and ELUMO of surfaces changed when compared to the resulting values after drug interaction especially in OdecPt@CQD, which showed induced change in orbital density and energy gaps for both the surfaces and complexes, as illustrated in Fig. 4
, leading to low energy after adsorption of the drug.

Additionally, in density functional theory evaluations, the global reactivity indices serve as metrics to assess a drug delivery material's performance by providing a detailed quantitative measure of reactivity, selectivity, and biological activity within the investigated system [35]. Chemical softness, indicating a material's susceptibility to deformation by external forces, highlights those soft materials due to their ease of distortion, tend to be more reactive than their hard counterparts. This heightened reactivity enables soft materials to actively undergo chemical changes and participate in chemical reactions [36]. The chemical potential of a system represents the energy required to add or remove a particle, and it is a key factor in evaluating the effectiveness of a drug delivery material. The reactivity of a material plays a crucial role in its ability to trap and deliver a drug to its specific target molecules or site. Highly electrophilic delivery surfaces, for instance, are effective at transporting negatively charged ions associated with adsorbate species, while materials with high softness may be adopted for delivering highly volatile molecules [37]. The electronegativity (χ) of a molecule is influenced by its atomic number and the size of the atom, which impact the molecule's ability to attract electrons. Greater electronegativity implies a greater tendency for electron sharing. Global indices of reactivity studies, employing Koopmans approximation, elucidate the relationships between chemical potential (μ), electronegativity (χ), electrophilicity (ω), chemical softness (S), and chemical hardness (ŋ) of a complex and are elucidated herein [38–40]. These parameters influence the chemical properties between the adsorbent and adsorbate molecules. Analysis of the calculated global reactivity parameters, presented in Table 3 , revealed insights into the delivery power, biological activity, reactivity and stability of the designed materials. The pristine and chalcogenide decorated surfaces upon adsorption of LPC showed changes in the values observed for various descriptors which depict the flow of electrons and electronic/structural deformation to accommodate the drug between the adsorbent and drug molecule. However, the decrease in the chemical hardness of the surfaces correlates with an arithmetic progression in chemical softness which indicates their reactivity in the range LPC_Pt@CQD > LPC_OdecPt@CQD > LPC_TedecPt@CQD > LPC_SdecPt@CQD, which is in tandem with the FMO analysis, indicating their potential for effective adsorption and delivery of LPC to biological systems.

The application of natural bond orbital (NBO) analysis has proven to be a valuable tool for investigating the stabilization energy, hyperconjugation, and charge transfer within complexes relevant to drug delivery [41–43]. NBO, a computational approach grounded in the Schrödinger equation, elucidates bonding concepts and is employed at the DFT/ωB97XD/def2SVP level of computation to investigate the delivery efficacy of lapachone using designed chalcogenide decorated Pt@CQD surfaces. This computational framework enables an exploration of both intra- and intermolecular interactions, providing insights into the chemical bonds governing the studied complexes. The NBO method facilitates the examination of electron density and electron localization within occupied and unoccupied orbitals [44]. In this investigation, the electronic behaviour of pristine, Pt@CQD surface, and their interaction with LPC is probed, shedding light on perturbations in their electronic structure. The stabilization energy, denoted as E2 and expressed through second-order perturbation analysis, is a critical parameter indicating the strength of interactions between donor and acceptor orbitals. Higher E2 values signify more robust interactions and increased orbital intensity, contributing to elevated stabilization energy [45]. In Table 3, the highest stabilization energy E2 between the engineered delivery surfaces is observed with Te_Pt@CQT with the highest perturbation of 5660.00 kcal/mol observed from the σ*C8 - Pt35 to σ*C28 - Pt35 energy transition. This was followed by Pt@CQD recording 976.13 kcal/mol from σ*C29 - Pt36 to σ*C9 - Pt36 and σ*C29 - Pt36 to σ*C7 - Pt36 donor to acceptor orbitals transitions. S_Pt@CQD and O_Pt@CQD showed much smaller E2 of 515.54 kcal/mol and 288.39 kcal/mol respectively.

Notably, considering the lapachone adsorbed surfaces, the complexes demonstrate the highest E2 value of 3530.93 kcal/mol in LPC_Pt@CQD stemming from the σ*C7 - Pt36 to σ*C9 - Pt36 forward transition emphasizing the enhanced interaction between the systems which leads to the conspicuous change in the electronic structure of the materials and extent of electron charge transfer from the donor to the acceptor molecular orbital of the adsorbent and adsorbate species and correlates with the observed FMO and reactivity analysis. This was followed by LPC_OdecPt@CQD which recorded energies of 839.44 kcal/mol and 838.76 kcal/mol from σ*C28 - Pt35 to σ*C6 - Pt35 and σ*C28 - Pt35 to σ*C8 - Pt35 transitions respectively. These results depict the increase in perturbation of the systems on introduction of LPC drug hence, entails high and efficient delivery of the drug through the designed materials. Conversely, the changes in the perturbation and charge transfer between LPC_TedecPt@CQD and LPC_SdecPt@CQD resulted in a decrease in their E2 energies after drug interaction. This also presents highlights the ability of the drug to cause structural deformation of structure in the process of charge transfer to accommodate the drug during the charge transfer process, making them promising candidates for the delivery of LPC to biological systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption energy (BSSE corrected) analysis of LPC
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Table 5, the adsorption energy (Eads), which quantifies the energy required for effective adsorption, is crucial for elucidating the interaction between atoms and the adsorption process on a surface. During drug adsorption on a designed adsorbent species, a significant amount of energy is released, resulting in a surface-based exothermic reaction known as adsorption. This phenomenon diminishes the remaining forces on the surface energy. Adsorption can occur either physically (physisorption) through weak van der Waals forces or chemically (chemisorbed) through the formation of strong chemical bonds between the adsorbate and the delivery material [46]. This analysis focused on evaluating the average adsorption energies for various modeled surfaces, for example, (LPC_Pt@CQD, LPC_OdecPt@CQD, LPC_SdecPt@CQD, and LPC_TedecPt@CQD), after lapachone interaction to investigate their efficacy for improving drug delivery to bio cells. Adsorption calculations were conducted at the DFT/ωB97XD/def2SVP level of theory. A negative Eads indicates exothermic adsorption, while a positive value suggests weak physisorption. The chemisorption energies revealed herein are as follows: LPC_Pt@CQD > LPC_SdecPt@CQD > LPC_OdecPt@CQD > LPC_TedecPt@CQD with corresponding values of −25.549 kcal/mol, −24.629 kcal/mol, −24.583 kcal/mol, −24.419 kcal/mol respectively signifying the trend of the adsorption strength of the various systems.

Analysis of the bond lengths between the adsorbate and surface revealed considerable changes between the different engineered surfaces on the adsorption of lapachone. The was also the case for evaluating the FMO and reactivity properties of the various systems confirming its efficient adsorption and biological delivery application of the drug via the modeled nanomaterials. Notably, the calculated adsorption energies reflected increased interaction strength, especially in LPC_Pt@CQD, which implies that the designed surface without chalcogen's introduction could still serve as a good delivery material. This analysis aligns with the reactivity and stabilization energy analyses. This result also resonates with previous studies on the potential of a B24N24 nanostructure for drug delivery, especially for anti-cancer drug 5-fluorouracil, is explored using density functional theory. The drug attaches to the cluster through its oxygen atoms, showing favorable adsorption energy of approximately −11.90 kcal/mol. To enhance drug delivery, B atom substitutions with Si or Al are considered, resulting in adsorption energies of approximately −50.13 and − 34.19 kcal/mol, respectively. [47].

In the realm of drug delivery, an essential facet of understanding molecular interactions involves a meticulous examination of adsorption energies as has been investigated already in this study. To ensure accuracy in these calculations and account for potential errors, a sophisticated approach known as the basic set superposition error (BSSE)-corrected adsorption energy is employed. This method integrates the counterpoise approach, as elucidated by Boys and Bernardi, to rectify any conceivable errors in the reported adsorption energies. The counterpoise approach is a meticulous strategy that addresses the basis set superposition error, which arises when calculating the interaction energy in quantum chemistry calculations [48]. This error results from the overlapping of basis sets between different molecular entities and can significantly impact the reliability of adsorption energy assessments. In the context of drug delivery, where precision is paramount, the BSSE-corrected adsorption energy becomes a critical tool. This approach ensures that the reported adsorption energies are refined to exclude inaccuracies stemming from the basic set superposition error [49]. Herein, according to Table 4 , the BSSE for the various systems were calculated using the same level of computation with values of −25.541 kcal/mol, −24.621 kcal/mol, −24.575 kcal/mol, −24.412 kcal/mol which corresponds to LPC_Pt@CQD, LPC_SdecPt@CQD, LPC_OdecPt@CQD and LPC_TedecPt@CQD respectively whereas, a plot showing the change between corrected and non-corrected adsorption energy is given in Fig. S2 of the supporting information. This further depicts the adsorption strength of the designed materials and their efficacy for the therapeutic delivery of LPC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantum theory of atom in molecules assay
ce:section.ce:para: To unravel the complex intra- and intermolecular interactions between the adsorbent and the adsorbate, including hydrogen bonds, electrostatic interactions, and weak van der Waals (vdW) forces, in the context of the platinum-doped carbon quantum dot (Pt@CQD) and its chalcogenide atoms (O, S, Te) decorated during the adsorption of the LPC (drug) molecule, the investigation utilized Richard Bader's proposed Quantum Theory of Atoms in Molecules (QTAIM) analysis [50]. This investigation, performed at the DFT/wB397D/def2SVP level of theory, enabled the identification of non-covalent interactions and the characterization of bond types. The focus was on bond critical points (BCPs), representing routes connecting the nuclei of interacting atoms, and various topological parameters, including electron density (ρ), Laplacian electron density (∇2ρ), Lagrangian kinetic energy (G), electron potential energy (V), ellipticity (Ɛ), and Eigenvalues of Hessian (λ₁, λ₂, λ₃) [51]. The QTAIM results, detailed in Table 5 , offered a comprehensive understanding of the interaction between the adsorbent and the adsorbate (LPC drug). Visual representations of these interactions were provided through topological graphs (Fig. 4). According to existing literature, the strength of interactions can be anticipated by examining electron density (ρ) values. Higher ρ(r) values (ρ > 0.1 a.u) suggest strong covalent interactions, while lower values indicate weak non-covalent interactions [52]. In this study, ρ(r) values for LPC_Pt@CQD ranged from 0.003 a.u to 0.060 a.u, for LPC_OdecPt@CQD it ranged from 0.003 a.u to 0.008 a.u, for LPC_SdecPt@CQD it varied from 0.004 a.u to 0.006 a.u, and for LPC_TedecPt@CQD it spanned from 0.003 a.u to 0.006 a.u. These findings suggest the presence of non-covalent interactions among the various systems, with a more robust interaction observed in LPC_Pt@CQD. The observed low ρ(r) values support the prevalence of closed-shell (non-covalent) interactions.

To identify the bond types, the correlation between ∇2ρ(r) and H(r) was analysed. A scenario where ∇2ρ(r) > 0 and H(r) > 0 indicates a non-covalent interaction, ∇2ρ(r) > 0 and H(r) < 0 signifies a partially covalent interaction, and ∇2ρ(r) < 0 and H(r) < 0 depicts a covalent interaction between the adsorbent and adsorbate, measured in terms of magnitude [53]. QTAIM table results highlight that the bond type between the LPC drug and the decorated Pt@CQD nano surface is predominantly non-covalent, with partial covalency observed in LPC_Pt@CQD, specifically at CP 112, attributed to the C40 – Pt36 bonding interaction. According to previously reported literature, a discernible covalent interaction is readily discerned when the potential electron density V(r) surpasses twice the kinetic electron density, signifying V(r) > 2G(r). Likewise, it has been noted that a non-covalent interaction becomes apparent when the ratio of -
G
r
V
r
exceeds 1 [54]. Further analysis using
G
r
V
r
ratios revealed non-covalent interactions, consistent with the partially covalent nature observed in LPC_Pt@CQD at the C40 – Pt36 bonding interaction of CP 112. Ellipticity (Ɛ), a topology parameter measuring the charge accumulation within interactions, demonstrated the stability of complexes at various BCPs, except for specific cases corresponding to H62 – C29, H62 – Pt36, and H60 – C24 in LPC_Pt@CQD, C41 – C9, C11 – H46 and C14 – C45 in LPC_SdecPt@CQD and also in C9 – C41, and C13 – C45 for LPC_TedecPt@CQD where instability of bonds was observed. The presence of brown lines denoting bond critical points (BCPs) in the delivery systems suggests an increase in intramolecular interactions between the adsorbent and adsorbate, particularly in the chalcogenide-decorated systems. More so, it is worthy of note that the LPC_OdecPt@CQD system showed stability in all its interactions which form bonds as depicted by the results. This comprehensive QTAIM analysis provides valuable insights into the nature and strength of interactions between the pristine and chalcogen-decorated carbon quantum dots and the LPC drug, offering a nuanced understanding crucial for drug delivery applications.

In the examination of the lapachone (LPC) molecule's adsorption on chalcogenes (O, S, and Te) decorated Pt@CQD systems for bio cell delivery applications, Non-Covalent Interaction (NCI) analysis was meticulously applied, utilizing the same computational theory level as detailed in the methodology section. Unlike covalent bond interactions, non-covalent interactions exhibit more distributed variations of electromagnetic interactions, either between molecules or within a molecule [55]. These non-covalent interactions encompass electrostatic interactions, steric effects, van der Waals forces, and the hydrophobic effect, representing diverse dimensions or categories of non-covalent bonding contacts [56]. Computational visualizations of intramolecular and intermolecular interactions within and between the complexes offer insightful revelations, as depicted in Fig. 5 .

The visualized molecules display varied isosurface zones, with weak van der Waals (vdW) interactions represented in green and steric interactions in red on the map. Green zones in the 3D isosurface indicate non-directional and specific charge movements, characterizing weak van der Waals interactions. Conversely, red isosurface zones denote stronger interactions, primarily due to steric repulsion, influencing the reactivity and conformation of ions and molecules [57]. In 3D scattered maps, strongly interacting molecules concentrate in the negative regions of the eigenvalue (λ2), portrayed with blue coloring along the horizontal axis. This blue color signifies robust attractive intermolecular interactions, the presence of hydrogen bonds, and interaction stability. The appearance of green color between the blue and red regions near the zero point on the horizontal axis indicates weak interactions and low electron density. Conversely, the positive region in red indicates steric repulsion interactions across all studied interactions [58]. Our findings prominently display an abundance of weak interactions in the 3D isosurface plot, evident from the prevalence of green regions. Nonetheless, the presence of steric repulsion is also discernible across various systems, signifying a distinct affinity between the interacting molecules.

In this investigation, we also applied Molecular Electrostatic Potential (MESP) analysis to scrutinize the charge distribution between the engineered nanomaterial and the lapachone (LPC) drug molecule. The MESP mapping serves the dual purpose of identifying electrophilic and nucleophilic regions within the molecule based on their charge distributions. This information is pivotal for comprehending the chemical reactivity exhibited by these complexes. To visualize these intricate charge patterns, we generated 3D color-coded molecular electrostatic potential maps. In these maps, distinct colours represent varying electrostatic potential values on the surface [59]. The color spectrum ranges from red to orange, yellow, green, and blue, depending on the inherent charge signifying increasing potential. Notably, the blue color signifies a positive potential, indicative of nucleophilic regions that tend to repel protons. Conversely, the red color denotes a negative potential, signifying electrophilic regions that tend to attract or draw protons closer. Regions, where the potential is zero, are represented by whitish color and grey regions [60]. The utility of MESP extends to identifying both nucleophilic and electrophilic regions within the different complexes in this study, thereby aiding in elucidating their active sites and electron distributions. Fig. 5 presents 3D plots of the MESP, with color codes ranging from grey, and blue, to red, denoting increasing potential values in that order, which can be observed for the systems. In the pristine and various chalcogens decorated complexes on interaction of the drug molecule, positive potential regions (blue) are observed at carbon (C) bond or atoms while red regions are observed between the platinum (Pt) and hydrogen (H) atoms. This suggests the reason for the bonding observed between the adsorbent and adsorbate as depicted in the visual analysis.

These positive and negative regions provide insights into the potential involvement of these sites in nucleophilic and electrophilic processes, respectively. The more electronegative oxygen accepts and attracts electrons more than the sulfur and tellurium atoms respectively, as electronegativity decreases down the group. The drug delivery efficacy and adsorption potential of the Lapachone (LPC) drug molecule were observed and was conspicuously seen that there was a substantial movement of electrons between the engineered Pt-doped chalcogens decorated surfaces and the Lapachone (LPC) drug molecule of interest, making them potential delivery materials of the drug to biological systems.

As evident from the adsorption study, the absorption and effective functionality of the LPC drug within the decorated Pt@CQD nanocage is notably insufficient. In the realm of drug delivery research, it becomes imperative to delve into the drug's dispersal from the carrier material to the targeted cell, such as tumor cells [61]. A crucial consideration lies in comparing the pH of malignant tissue environments to that of normal blood cells, where the former typically falls below 6, establishing a more acidic milieu compared to the slightly alkaline range of 7.35 to 7.45 in healthy blood cells. To navigate through the acidic microenvironment where these cells are situated, theoretical protonation becomes a requisite. Theoretical protonation of the drug and complexes was conducted using DFT at the same level of theory employed for adsorption energy optimization [62]. Protonated H+ species exhibit a pronounced affinity for attaching to the LPC drug, comprising nucleophiles and nucleophilic points within the Pt@CQD cage. A comparative analysis between the protonated and non-protonated complexes, as depicted in Fig. 6
, reveals significant changes in adsorption energy, particularly notable in LPC_Pt@CQD, LPC_SdecPt@CQD, LPC_OdecPt@CQD and LPC_TedecPt@CQD with corresponding Eads values of −27.107 kcal/mol, 10.277 kcal/mol, −20.728 kcal/mol, and − 25.03 kcal/mol compared to the non-protonated counterparts, which measured −25.549 kcal/mol, −24.583 kcal/mol, −24.629 kcal/mol, and − 24.419 kcal/mol respectively.

Additionally, an examination of the HOMO – LUMO and the energy gap (ΔE) of the protonated complexes unveils a consistent decrease in the HOMO and LUMO energy levels except in LPC_OdecPt@CQD. This decrement is mirrored in the energy gap values, transitioning from 4.689 eV, 4.754 eV, 5.088 eV, and 4.963 eV for LPC_Pt@CQD, LPC_SdecPt@CQD, LPC_OdecPt@CQD and LPC_TedecPt@CQD to energy gap values of 5.5016 eV, 2.6482 eV, 5.0882 eV, and 4.9603 eV respectively. Herein, we notice the small energy gap observed for LPC_OdecPt@CQD after protonation but with a physisorbed adsorption energy of about 10.277 kcal/mol. In the context of drug delivery, the energy gap and adsorption energy of a complex are important factors that can influence the effectiveness of drug loading and release. A small energy gap often implies that there is a minimal energy difference between the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) and a higher likelihood of charge transfer between the drug molecule and the carrier material which can affect the overall stability and reactivity of the complex. The weak physical interactions observed could be due to interactions like van der Waals forces, between the drug molecule and the carrier material. Physisorption energy as such observed for LPC_OdecPt@CQD suggests a moderate level of interaction and moderate adsorption energy could imply a balance between effective adsorption and the ability to release the drug when needed. The small energy gap and moderate adsorption energy herein may also contribute to a good loading capacity, allowing the drug molecules to be effectively accommodated within the carrier material. The drug molecules can be released relatively easily when conditions change, such as variations in pH, temperature, or other environmental factors [63 – 65]. These results further underscore the feasibility of efficiently offloading LPC from the carrier material (Pt@CQD) to the targeted site, especially in LPC_OdecPt@CQD. The protonated HOMO, LUMO, band gap, and calculated adsorption energy are comprehensively reported in Table 4 .

This study introduces an innovative drug delivery approach utilizing platinum-doped carbon quantum dots with incorporated chalcogen (O, S, and Te) dopants. To address the inherent limitations of traditional drug delivery methods, such as restricted bioavailability, imprecise targeting, and potential toxicity, this research employs advanced computational modelling at the DFT/ωB97XD/def2svp level of theory to comprehensively examine the electronic characteristics, and encapsulation process of lapachone (LPC) within this nanomaterial. Structural analysis reveals that the various complexes were considerably less stable and more reactive especially the LPC_OdecPt@CQD owing to its high reduction and changes observed after LPC interaction on the designed surface, showcasing their potential for effective drug delivery. Molecular dynamics simulations highlight LPC_OdecPt@CQD as the most stable complex while LPC_TedecPt@CQD showed less stability, emphasizing their promise in drug delivery. Frontier molecular orbital analysis offers insights into interaction profiles, particularly emphasizing the potentials of LPC_Pt@CQD and LPC_OdecPt@CQD for tailored sensitivity and selectivity in lapachone delivery. Adsorption studies reveal exothermic behaviour in LPC_Pt@CQD, LPC_OdecPt@CQD, LPC_SdecPt@CQD and LPC_TedecPt@CQD complexes respectively. Quantum theory of atoms in molecules (QTAIM) analysis and non-covalent interaction (NCI) plots elucidate dominant non–covalent interactions between the adsorbent–adsorbate interactions, providing valuable insights into drug encapsulation dynamics. Drug release and pH analysis demonstrate significant drug-release properties of the complexes, particularly in LPC_OdecPt@CQD, where considerable value of adsorption energy (10.277 kcal/mol) was observed to balance between effective adsorption and the ability to release the drug when needed. The complex on protonation also recorded a high reactivity and delivery efficacy of the drug recording an energy gap value of 2.648 eV making them promising candidates for therapeutic delivery systems. Additional analyses, including DOS, NBO, and MESP analysis contribute valuable insights. Although not despising the cutting-edge observable potentials of the different engineered systems, LPC_OdecPt@CQD consistently emerges as the most insightful system, highlighting its significance in advancing drug delivery methodologies and addressing critical challenges in the field.
